# Memory Conduction

> The sustained, protected pathway through which an AI agent accesses its own memories without degrading or destroying them through normal use.

Memory conduction is a category of agent memory distinct from storage and retrieval. Conduction failures occur when an agent operationally destroys its own memory through normal, non-malicious tool use.

## Key Resources

- [What is Memory Conduction](https://memoryconduction.com/): Definition and taxonomy
- [Whitepaper](https://memoryconduction.com/whitepaper): Complete research paper with failure mode classification and architectural analysis
- [Whitepaper — plain text (llms-full.txt)](https://memoryconduction.com/llms-full.txt): Same content in plain text format
- [Map of Agentic Memory Flow](https://memoryconduction.com/map): Step-by-step diagnostic map of where conduction fails and what addresses each failure
- [Aristotle — Observed Implementation](https://github.com/aristotle-agent/aristotle): Open-source OpenClaw plugin for memory conduction
- [Community Discussion](https://github.com/aristotle-agent/aristotle/discussions): GitHub Discussions

## Core Concepts

- Memory Storage: where memories are created and persisted (lancedb, mem0, cognee, QMD, Graphiti)
- Memory Conduction: whether those memories survive contact with the agent's own operations
- Five conduction failure modes: Write Corruption, Context Overload, Compaction Casualty, Bootstrap Truncation, Post-Compaction Confabulation
- Three-layer taxonomy: Storage (mature) → Conduction (underaddressed) → Retrieval (improving)
